mnemonics.title: L-SEARCH: Look Sequentially Every Element Checking All
mnemonics.content: Linear Search checks each element one by one from the beginning until the target is found or list ends. Mnemonic: 'L-SEARCH' reminds you it searches sequentially like checking each door in a hallway.

mnemonics.title: B-SEARCH: Bisect Search Space By Cutting Half
mnemonics.content: Binary Search divides the sorted array in half each iteration. Mnemonic: 'B-SEARCH' = Bisect and Search - always cut the search space in half to find the target faster.

mnemonics.title: LO-HI-MID: Low Keeps Dropping, High Keeps Climbing, Middle Guides
mnemonics.content: In Binary Search, maintain low and high pointers, calculate mid. If target < mid, move high to mid-1; if target > mid, move low to mid+1. Mnemonic: 'L-H-M' reminds you of the three pointers always used.

mnemonics.title: SPLIT: Sorted array, Pick midpoint, Locate target, Iterate, Find/Not Find
mnemonics.content: Binary Search requirements: 1) Sorted array 2) Pick midpoint 3) Compare with target 4) Iterate until found or low > high. Mnemonic: 'S-P-L-I-T' for the workflow steps.

acronyms.usage: Use when array is unsorted or small. Time: O(n), Space: O(1)
Use only on sorted arrays. Time: O(log n), Space: O(1) iterative / O(log n) recursive
Boundary markers in Binary Search that define the current search range. Low starts at 0, High at n-1
Calculated as low + (high-low)/2 to avoid integer overflow. Used to divide search space in Binary Search
In Linear Search: check n elements worst case. In Binary Search: only log2(n) comparisons needed

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hallway with numbered doors. You start at door 1, check if your friend is behind it, then move to door 2, then 3... until found. Like checking each locker in a school corridor.

Think of guessing a number between 1-100. You ask 'Is it >50?' If yes, guess 75, then ask 'Is it >75?' Each question cuts possibilities in half. Visualize a book index where you open to middle, then decide which half contains your word.

Draw a number line 1-16. For target 13: first mid=8 (too low) → new range 9-16; mid=12 (too low) → range 13-16; mid=14 (too high) → range 13-13; found! Visualize the range shrinking like a rubber band.
Picture two fingers marking low and high on a ruler. The middle finger (mid) points to the center. Depending on comparison, slide the fingers inward - like narrowing down a page in a dictionary.

analogies.analogy: Looking for a specific book in an unsorted library by checking each shelf, one book at a time. Or searching for a contact in a phone by scrolling through all contacts alphabetically.
Looking up a word in a dictionary by opening to the middle, then deciding whether to search the left or right half. Or finding a page in a phone book by starting from the middle and repeating.

Like the 'hot or cold' game where someone hides an object and you ask 'hotter or colder?' Each answer eliminates half the search area. Or finding a word in a dictionary: you don't start from A every time!

Binary Search only works on sorted data like searching a sorted deck of cards. You need the phone book to be alphabetically sorted to use the divide-and-conquer approach; unsorted, you'd have to check every entry.
Linear Search is like walking up stairs one step at a time. Binary Search is like taking an elevator that skips half the floors each time - much faster for tall buildings (large datasets).
